hard.explanation: The 'defer' attribute tells the browser to execute the script after the document has been parsed, in order.

hard.question: When using the <picture> element, which child tag is used to declare multiple media queries and image sources for responsive designs?
hard.explanation: The element contains zero or more elements and one element to offer alternative versions of an image based on media conditions.

hard.question: What is the purpose of the 'rel="noopener"' attribute when combined with target="_blank" on an anchor tag?
options: It improves SEO indexing by search engine crawlers
It prevents the newly opened page from accessing the original window via window.opener
It forces the browser to cache the target page for performance
It ensures the target site opens over a secure HTTPS connection
hard.explanation: Using rel="noopener" is an important security practice that prevents the opened page from exploiting 'window.opener' to maliciously control or redirect the original page.

hard.question: Which attribute on an <audio> or <video> element ensures that the media resource will start downloading in the background as soon as the page loads, even if it doesn't autoplay?
hard.explanation: The preload attribute accepts 'auto', hinting to the browser that it should begin buffering the media file for a smooth user experience when playback starts.

hard.question: What is the meaning and utility of the 'contenteditable' attribute in HTML?
options: It identifies content that search engine optimization scripts can modify
It makes any standard HTML element interactive and editable by the end user directly in the browser
It marks a section of text as dynamically translatable by screen readers
It permits developers to write code inside code blocks that changes in real-time
hard.explanation: The contenteditable attribute turns an element into an editable rich text area where users can add, delete, or modify text within the live view.

hard.question: Which of the following describes the difference between the 'async' and 'defer' attributes on script tags?
options: 'async' locks the UI thread; 'defer' handles processing in a separate background web worker context
'async' loads scripts out of order as soon as they download; 'defer' guarantees execution in the exact order they appear in the markup after parsing
'async' only works for inline scripts; 'defer' only works for external scripts
There is no difference; modern browsers treat them identically
hard.explanation: Both download scripts without blocking the HTML parser. However, 'async' scripts execute the moment they finish downloading (interrupting parsing and ordering), while 'defer' scripts wait for the document parser to finish and maintain order.

hard.question: What does the 'srcset' attribute do inside an tag?
options: It establishes a fallback path array if the primary image returns a 404 error code
It defines a list of varying image resource files alongside width or pixel-density descriptors for responsive image selection
It sets up a series of alternating filter styles managed via standard CSS frameworks
It defines pre-compiled spritesheets for canvas animation renderings
hard.explanation: The 'srcset' attribute provides the browser with a comma-separated list of image sources, allowing the browser to dynamically choose the optimal image based on device display density and screen size.

hard.question: For web accessibility (a11y), what does the 'aria-live' attribute dictate when set to 'assertive'?
options: It forces video streams to render at higher frame rates for visibility
It tells screen readers to interrupt whatever they are currently reading to announce updates immediately
It prevents users from altering input focus using the tab key
It highlights key form elements using high contrast border colors
hard.explanation: aria-live="assertive" is used for critical updates. It tells assistive technologies to immediately clear the speech queue and notify the user of dynamic changes to that section of the page.

hard.question: What happens if an HTML form contains multiple submit inputs, and the user presses 'Enter' inside a text field to submit the form?
options: The form submission fails entirely due to ambiguous design definitions
The browser submits the form using the name/value pairing of the first submit button encountered in the DOM
The browser prompts a UI modal popup asking the user to choose their preferred button target
The form payload submits all button names and values combined together
hard.explanation: Implicit form submission (pressing Enter) automatically treats the first valid submit element encountered in the DOM sequence as the trigger, appending its specific name and value parameters.

hard.question: What is the primary architectural purpose of the HTML5 <template> tag?
options: To hold client-side content that is completely hidden from user view and ignored by the parser until instantiated via JavaScript
To layout structural wireframes that serve as master page boundaries during development processing tasks
To download and run external macro assets using background CSS pre-processors
To generate a hardcoded UI theme template framework overlay without writing script bindings
hard.explanation: The <template> tag acts as a mechanism for holding HTML content that is not rendered on page load, but can be safely parsed, cloned, and inserted into the runtime DOM later using JavaScript.

easy.question: What does CSS stand for?
easy.explanation: CSS stands for Cascading Style Sheets, used to style HTML elements.

easy.question: Which HTML tag is used to link an external CSS file?
easy.explanation: The <link> tag inside the <head> section is used to connect an external stylesheet to an HTML document.

easy.question: Which CSS property is used to change the background color of an element?
easy.explanation: The 'background-color' property sets the background color of an element, while 'color' handles text color.

easy.question: What is the correct syntax to change the text color of all paragraph elements to red?
easy.explanation: In CSS, element selectors are written directly without angle brackets, followed by declarations wrapped in curly braces.

easy.question: Which character is used to select elements with a specific class name in CSS?
easy.explanation: A period or dot (.) is used to define and target a class selector in CSS.

easy.question: Which character is used to select an element with a specific ID in CSS?
easy.explanation: The hash or pound sign (#) is used to define and target an ID selector in CSS.

easy.question: How do you make text bold using CSS properties?
easy.explanation: The 'font-weight' property configures how thick or thin characters in text should be displayed.

easy.question: How do you add a comment in a CSS file?
easy.explanation: CSS comments begin with /* and end with */, and can span across multiple lines.

easy.question: Which CSS property is used to add space inside an element's border (inner spacing)?
easy.explanation: The 'padding' property creates space around an element's content, inside of any defined borders.

easy.question: What is the default value of the position property in CSS?
easy.explanation: HTML elements are positioned 'static' by default, meaning they follow the normal flow of the page layout.

medium.question: Which CSS property controls the text size?
medium.explanation: The 'font-size' property sets the size of the text.

medium.question: What is the correct order of the shorthand 'margin' property values representing top, right, bottom, and left?
medium.explanation: The shorthand layout properties in CSS follow a clockwise sequence starting from the top: Top, Right, Bottom, Left (TRBL).

medium.question: According to the standard CSS Box Model, what components make up the total calculated width of an element?
medium.explanation: By default (content-box), an element's visible width is calculated as Content width + left/right padding + left/right border layout dimensions.

medium.question: Which CSS property is used to change the mouse cursor shape when hovering over an element?
medium.explanation: The 'cursor' property specifies the type of mouse cursor to show when pointing over an element.

medium.question: In a Flexbox container, which property handles horizontal alignment along the main axis when the flex-direction is set to row?
medium.explanation: The 'justify-content' property aligns flex items along the main axis of the current line of the flex container.

medium.question: Which CSS property allows you to round the corners of an element's outer border edge?
medium.explanation: The 'border-radius' property lets developers define how rounded the outer corners of an element's box should be.

medium.question: What is the key functional difference between using 'display: none;' and 'visibility: hidden;'?
options: 'display: none;' leaves the visual space empty; 'visibility: hidden;' collapses the elements structurally
'display: none;' structurally removes the element from the layout flow; 'visibility: hidden;' hides the element but preserves its physical layout space
There is no difference; both function identically across modern layouts
'display: none;' only works on text blocks; 'visibility: hidden;' targets structural containers
medium.explanation: 'display: none' eliminates the element entirely from the page layout space, whereas 'visibility: hidden' hides it visually while leaving the blank container box allocation intact.

medium.question: Which CSS selector selects all <a> elements that are placed immediately after a <div> element?
medium.explanation: The adjacent sibling combinator (+) targets an element that directly follows another specified element.

medium.question: What does the 'em' relative unit represent when assigning a font-size to an element?
options: The current viewport scale width matrix percentage multiplier
The font-size of the element's parent container context
The absolute physical size baseline calibrated against printer device layouts
The specific rendering width of the character capital letter M
medium.explanation: An 'em' unit is relative to the font-size of its direct parent element. If used on the font-size property itself, it relates to the parent's font size.

medium.question: Which pseudo-class is used to target and style an anchor link that the user has already clicked and loaded?
medium.explanation: The ':visited' pseudo-class targets links that have already been accessed by the current browser user history.

hard.question: What is the specificity value of an ID selector in CSS?
hard.explanation: In CSS specificity calculation, an ID selector has a value of 100 (in the standard 0-0-0-0 format: 0-1-0-0).

hard.question: Given the rule 'box-sizing: border-box;', how does the browser determine the actual content layout width of an element?
options: It scales the given width parameter dynamically according to the parent view metrics
It subtracts the defined padding and border sizes from the explicit overall width property
It appends the assigned padding and border sizes directly on top of the width value
It skips border computations entirely to favor responsive text rendering spaces
hard.explanation: When 'border-box' is active, the width property defines the overall layout box boundary. The browser automatically shrinks the internal content space by subtracting padding and border dimensions.

hard.question: Which of the following grid layout properties is used to create responsive columnar distributions automatically without relying heavily on explicit @media queries?
hard.explanation: Using 'repeat' alongside 'auto-fit' or 'auto-fill' combined with a 'minmax()' limit calculation forces columns to wrap intelligently into structural boundaries autonomously.

hard.question: What happens when an element is assigned a position: absolute value?
options: It tracks viewports autonomously ignoring scrolling coordinates completely
It positions relative to its closest parent container with a position property other than static
It aligns exclusively against the absolute origin boundary coordinates of the main body element context
It locks layout interaction preventing adjacent text blocks from shifting dimensions
hard.explanation: Absolute elements remove themselves from normal document flow. They shift position matching coordinate definitions matching the bounds of their nearest non-static positioned ancestor wrapper.

hard.question: Which CSS property is required to clip element overflow content and enable rendering smooth custom graphic adjustments using the 'mask-image' mechanism?
hard.explanation: The 'clip-path' property constructs specific bounding shapes that hide any parts of an element lying outside the designated vector path boundaries.

hard.question: What is the primary function of the 'will-change' property in CSS?
options: It signals to the browser engine ahead of time which properties are expected to animate, allowing GPU layer optimizations
It forces standard browser applications to update runtime variable parameters globally
It overrides structural declaration overrides avoiding unexpected responsive layout bugs
It tracks framework dependencies during complex runtime state updates
hard.explanation: The 'will-change' property acts as a performance hint for browsers, alerting them to isolate elements onto separate graphic rendering layers (GPU) before heavy transition processing kicks off.

hard.question: What layout container reference base is used to scale dimensions when specifying dimensions with 'svh' units versus standard 'vh' units?
options: The scaled variation vector baseline matching typography tracking variables
The smallest possible viewport height frame dimension that accounts for dynamic browser navigation chrome bars
The strict root context container dimension factoring parent overflow blocks
The server-side virtual layout template tracking configuration matrix
hard.explanation: The 'svh' relative unit refers to the 'Small Viewport Height', adjusting dynamically when mobile browser navigation bars expand or retract, preventing content from bleeding off-screen.

hard.question: How do styles declared with the '@layer' directive resolve priority differences when conflicting selectors appear outside layered definitions?
options: Styles inside named layers always override unlayered standard rules by default
Unlayered style declarations always maintain higher cascade priority over rules organized inside @layer containers
The browser flips resolution logic, choosing rules that appear earliest in code parsing cycles
The framework falls back onto structural tag names ignoring user specificity overrides
hard.explanation: In the CSS Cascade layers architecture, standard unlayered styles maintain higher precedence over styles nested inside '@layer' contexts, regardless of selector specificity matching.

hard.question: Which flexbox property combination can safely prevent individual flex items from shrinking out of proportion when parent layouts compress?
hard.explanation: By explicitly configuring 'flex-shrink: 0;', the layout engine prevents a flex item from dropping below its calculated or set base width dimensions.

hard.question: What is the specific behavior of the position: sticky; configuration value?
options: It forces layout containers to anchor to their original coordinates, completely breaking page flow
It treats elements as static until they cross a predefined scroll threshold boundary, where they toggle into a fixed orientation within their parent box container
It restricts item layouts to rendering strictly within independent inline blocks
It locks text blocks inside structural canvas backgrounds avoiding dynamic browser zoom transformations
hard.explanation: 'position: sticky' acts like a hybrid between static and fixed placement. The element tracks standard scroll flow until its parent container boundaries hit defined parameters (like top: 0), adhering to that position until the parent boundary passes out of viewport view.

easy.question: Which symbol is used for comments in JavaScript?
easy.explanation: // is used for single-line comments; /* */ is used for multi-line comments.

easy.question: Which keyword is used to declare a variable that cannot be reassigned?
easy.explanation: Variables declared using the 'const' keyword cannot be reassigned a new value after definition.

easy.question: What is the correct way to write an alert box in JavaScript?
easy.explanation: The global built-in method 'alert()' displays an alert dialog box with specified content.

easy.question: How do you define a standard named function in JavaScript?
easy.explanation: A named function declaration begins with the 'function' keyword, followed by the function name, parentheses, and curly brackets.

easy.question: How do you round the number 7.25 to the nearest whole integer?
easy.explanation: Math.round() rounds a number up or down to the nearest mathematical whole integer based on fractional values.

easy.question: Which array method adds one or more elements to the end of an array?
easy.explanation: The push() method mutates an array by appending elements directly to its end. unshift() adds items to the beginning.

easy.question: What is the correct syntax for opening a basic 'for' loop in JavaScript?
easy.explanation: A standard for loop requires initialization expression, termination conditional statement, and update expression separated by semicolons.

easy.question: Which data type represents true or false logical valuations in JavaScript?
easy.explanation: The Boolean data type natively tracks truthy logic flags explicitly representing true or false value states.

easy.question: How do you select an HTML element with the id 'demo' using JavaScript DOM manipulation?
easy.explanation: The document.getElementById() method returns the singular DOM element matching the specified ID selector string value.

easy.question: What event fires when a user interacts with an HTML button element via mouse click?
easy.explanation: The click event (triggered inline via the attribute 'onclick') fires when a pointer device is clicked and released over an element.

medium.question: What does the '===' operator check in JavaScript?
medium.explanation: The strict equality operator (===) checks both value and data type without type coercion.

medium.question: What is the output of the following statement: console.log(typeof NaN);
medium.explanation: Although 'NaN' stands for 'Not-a-Number', its structural data type representation within ECMAScript specs is mathematically categorized as a 'number'.

medium.question: Which array method builds a brand-new array by processing and transforming every single item inside an existing array?
medium.explanation: The map() method iterates through an array, executes a callback loop function on each value, and returns a new populated array with converted records.

medium.question: What is the exact functional distinction between variable tracking behaviors of 'let' and 'var' declarations?
options: 'var' cannot be reassigned; 'let' values can alter over execution runs
'let' is globally injected onto window layers; 'var' remains safe from leaks
There are no semantic variances since modern compilers process them identically
medium.explanation: 'let' binds scopes to the immediate bounding statement block {}, while 'var' hoists expressions to parent functional bounds.

medium.question: What is the evaluated outcome of expression statement: console.log('5' + 3);
medium.explanation: When adding a string and a numeric type, JavaScript performs type coercion, casting numbers into strings, transforming addition operations into string concatenation.

medium.question: Which option represents the correct structure of an ES6 arrow function framework?
medium.explanation: Arrow functions leverage a concise initialization syntax removing explicit keywords via assignment operators and arrow symbols: () => {}.

medium.question: What does the statement 'this' commonly reference inside a standard method running directly inside an object context?
options: The global runtime window window wrapper element
The parent object containing and invoking the running method
The scope trace containing independent sub-routine elements
An internal index mapping variable variables
medium.explanation: When executing a method bound inside an object context, 'this' references the operational target object instance resolving invocations.

medium.question: What does a JavaScript Promise return when it has completed successfully?
options: A pending status update placeholder loop
A resolved value delivered via .then() callback handlers
An execution trace throwing handling exceptions
medium.explanation: Promises transition across asynchronous states. Successful handling fulfills execution pipelines, triggering .then() bindings returning targeted payloads.

medium.question: Which method cancels a repetitive interval timing tracker initialized via a setInterval() statement loop?
medium.explanation: The global method clearInterval() stops repeated executions by passing the numeric identifier reference returned from the initial setInterval() setup.

medium.question: What method is used to serialize a standard JavaScript object into an exchangeable JSON string format string sequence?
medium.explanation: JSON.stringify() converts objects or literal elements into compliant JSON string structural notations. JSON.parse() parses strings back into native objects.

hard.question: What is the output of: console.log(typeof []);
hard.explanation: In JavaScript, arrays are a type of object, so typeof [] returns 'object'.

hard.question: What describes a JavaScript closure?
options: The execution pipeline that forces memory leaks inside garbage collectors
A function combined with lexical environment references to its surrounding outer function variable records
A runtime structure locking objects to ensure scope parameters cannot alter later
The termination hook triggered when conditional loops hit exit limits
hard.explanation: A closure gives an inner function persistent entry pathways tracking variables from outer sibling functional execution boundaries, even after parent runs close.

hard.question: What occurs during the macro-task parsing stage when the JavaScript engine processes code tracking a variable inside the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)?
options: Variables evaluate automatically as undefined statements
The program immediately halts execution throwing a ReferenceError
The engine falls back to evaluating global window parameters instead
The property quietly mutates internal prototype arrays safely
hard.explanation: Variables declared via let and const exist in the Temporal Dead Zone from the beginning of block parsing until their explicit execution assignment statement lines run. Accessing them inside TDZ bounds throws an immediate ReferenceError.

hard.question: What is the output of the following tracking statement: console.log(0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3);
hard.explanation: JavaScript uses IEEE 754 floating-point math benchmarks. Floating-point precision fractions lack clean binary representations, resulting in 0.1 + 0.2 evaluating to 0.30000000000000004, making comparison calculations return false.

hard.question: What is the precise behavioral difference between utilizing Function.prototype.call and Function.prototype.bind?
options: 'call' forces script errors; 'bind' creates background asynchronous execution contexts
'call' executes functions instantly providing arguments individually; 'bind' returns a new bound function wrapper that can execute later
'call' passes parameter variables packed into arrays; 'bind' restricts logic to object variables exclusively
There is no functional processing difference between either method configuration definition
hard.explanation: Both alter 'this' pointer references. However, .call() executes the function target immediately, while .bind() generates a new function copy with fixed contextual pointers bound for delayed future execution.

hard.question: What structural event propagation sequence describes 'Event Bubbling' inside DOM event handler trees?
options: Events drop vertically downwards from window roots target filtering layers down onto specific element triggers
Events execute on target nodes, then propagate sequentially upwards through parent structural elements to root nodes
Events run parallel paths simultaneously across unrelated layout node elements
Events dispatch exclusively once to window frames without evaluating inline children layers
hard.explanation: Event bubbling triggers handlers starting directly on the innermost target component, then ascends through ancestor HTML element tags until hitting root document containers.

hard.question: What is the output of the code block layout sequence tracking variable reference behaviors: let a = [1, 2]; let b = a; b.push(3); console.log(a);
hard.explanation: Arrays use reference-based tracking pointers. Setting variable b to equal a duplicates the underlying memory reference pointer location instead of cloning the data structures, meaning alterations to b mutate the source array shared by variable a.

hard.question: Which mechanism enables JavaScript objects to inherit properties and methods from other object instances natively?
options: Classical functional class compiler abstraction systems
Prototypal inheritance tracking hidden internal link structures
Async callback injection matrix processing functions
hard.explanation: JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance models. Objects link to internal prototype references ([[Prototype]]), searching up ancestor chains to execute traits or read parameters missing on current objects.

hard.question: What does the 'new' keyword actually perform under the hood when executing structural object instantiation calls against functions?
options: It converts functional execution loops into asynchronous threads
It builds a blank object, sets its internal prototype link to the constructor function's prototype property, and binds the context instance as 'this'
It prevents garbage collection trackers from removing active methods from memory runs
It transforms text objects into serialized database reference points automatically
hard.explanation: The 'new' keyword initiates object instantiation by building a plain object, binding prototype pointers, mapping 'this' contextual references to the new container instance, and returning that instance implicitly.

hard.question: What is the main task allocation purpose of the JavaScript Event Loop environment configuration architecture?
options: To execute heavy math operations on individual threads concurrently
To monitor execution stack traces and handle queue item tasks sequentially once the primary call stack empties
To fetch external script dependencies automatically before DOM parsing states conclude
To track layout transformations avoiding CSS component alignment layout errors
hard.explanation: JavaScript is single-threaded. The event loop acts as a structural manager, monitoring the main call stack, and when that stack clears, pulling tasks out from macro/micro task callback queues to execute safely.

easy.question: What is React primarily used for?
easy.explanation: React is a JavaScript library for building reusable UI components, primarily for single-page applications.

easy.question: What extension is commonly used to combine JavaScript with HTML-like tags in React code?
easy.explanation: JSX stands for JavaScript XML. It allows developers to write HTML elements directly in JavaScript files, making component structures intuitive.

easy.question: How are properties (data) passed from a parent component down to a child component?
easy.explanation: Props (short for properties) are read-only objects passed down from parent components to child components to share data.

easy.question: In React, data flows in which structural direction?
easy.explanation: React uses unidirectional data flow, meaning data moves down from parent components to child components, keeping debugging simple and predictable.

easy.question: What is the correct syntax to render a functional component named 'MyButton' inside JSX?
easy.explanation: React components must start with a capital letter and are rendered in JSX using standard self-closing or matching XML-style tags.

easy.question: What command is commonly used to spin up a basic development server for a standard Vite-based React application?
easy.explanation: In modern React setups using bundlers like Vite, running 'npm run dev' boots up the local development environment.

easy.question: Which of the following is true about React state?
options: It is managed outside the component and is read-only
It is internal data that can change over time and belongs specifically to that component
It cannot be altered once the app initializes
It is synchronized with external databases automatically
easy.explanation: State is a built-in object used to contain data that might change over the component's life cycle. When state changes, the component re-renders.

easy.question: How do you render a dynamic JavaScript expression or variable inside a JSX markup block?
options: Wrap it in double curly braces {{}}
Wrap it in single curly braces {}
Prefix it with a dollar sign $
easy.explanation: Curly braces {} are used in JSX to evaluate and render inline JavaScript expressions, variables, or functions directly inside markup layout elements.

easy.question: What is the primary function of the Virtual DOM in React?
options: To duplicate the entire browser window interface for backups
To keep a lightweight representation of the real DOM in memory and sync efficiently via reconciliation
To encrypt web layouts against client security vulnerabilities
To load images faster on mobile devices
easy.explanation: React keeps a virtual copy of the UI in memory. When changes occur, it differences the two and updates only the necessary elements in the actual DOM, optimizing performance.

easy.question: What special attribute is required when rendering arrays of components dynamically using a map() function loop?
easy.explanation: The 'key' attribute is a special string attribute you need to include when creating lists of elements. Keys help React identify which items have changed, been added, or been removed.

medium.question: Which hook is used to manage state in a functional React component?
medium.explanation: useState is the primary hook for adding state to functional components.

medium.question: Which hook is used to perform side effects, such as data fetching or DOM manipulation, in functional components?
medium.explanation: The useEffect hook lets you perform side effects in functional components, serving a similar purpose to lifecycle methods in class components.

medium.question: What does a blank dependency array `[]` passed to a `useEffect` hook signify?
options: The effect runs on every single component re-render loop
The effect runs exactly once when the component mounts
The effect is disabled and will never run
The effect runs only when the component error bounds trigger
medium.explanation: Providing an empty dependency array tells React that the effect doesn't depend on any values from props or state, so it executes only on the initial mount.

medium.question: How should you correctly update a state variable named 'count' using its setter function 'setCount' if the new value depends on the previous state value?
medium.explanation: When the new state depends on the previous state, you should pass a functional updater callback to ensure you are working with the most up-to-date state value across batching operations.

medium.question: What occurs when you update a React component's state variable directly without using its designated setter function (e.g., `myState = 'newValue'`)?
options: The app crashes with an explicit compiler error
The value changes internally but React will not trigger a re-render to update the user interface
React re-renders the component but skips updating sub-children
The value is automatically synced using deep proxies anyway
medium.explanation: Mutating state directly bypasses React's state management lifecycle, meaning React doesn't notice the adjustment and won't trigger the necessary UI update re-renders.

medium.question: What is the main benefit of using the `useContext` hook across an expansive component tree structure?
options: It automatically saves component states to browser cookies
It allows you to share data globally across components without manually passing props through every intermediary layer (prop drilling)
It enforces faster CSS layout style tracking and compilation rules
It speeds up background network API payload parsing operations
medium.explanation: Context provides a way to pass data down the component tree without having to drill props down manually through every nested element level.

medium.question: What built-in feature is used to capture javascript errors anywhere in a child component tree and render a fallback UI instead of crashing the app?
options: try/catch code blocks wrapped around JSX elements
Error Boundaries implemented via class component methods
The useError hook tracking loop arrays
The Global App Error fallback configuration switch
medium.explanation: Error Boundaries are React components that catch JavaScript errors anywhere in their child component tree, log those errors, and display a fallback UI.

medium.question: What is the purpose of returning a clean-up function at the end of a execution inside a `useEffect` hook?
options: To delete the component state and clear operational caches completely
To clean up side effects (like clearing event listeners or timers) when the component unmounts or before re-running the effect
To signal to the compiler that optimization cycles are complete
To force adjacent tracking sibling components to reload details
medium.explanation: The cleanup function runs when the component unmounts or before the effect re-runs due to a dependency update, preventing memory leaks (e.g., clearing active intervals or event listeners).

medium.question: What is a major advantage of utilizing React Fragments (`<React.Fragment>` or `<>`)?
options: They apply global styling adjustments automatically without CSS rules
They allow you to group a list of children elements without adding extra unnecessary nodes to the actual DOM layout tree
They convert standard functional components into asynchronous threads
They double the rendering performance speed metrics of tracking nodes
medium.explanation: Fragments let you group a list of children elements without adding extra wrapper nodes (like an unwanted extra `<div>`) to the final rendered DOM markup structure.

medium.question: Which hook can be used to persist a mutable value across renders without triggering a component re-render when that value updates?
medium.explanation: The useRef hook returns a mutable ref object whose `.current` property persists across renders. Crucially, updating a ref does not trigger a component re-render loop.

hard.question: What is the purpose of React.memo()?
options: To prevent unnecessary re-renders of a component
hard.explanation: React.memo() is a higher-order component that memoizes a component, re-rendering it only when its props change.

hard.question: What is the key optimization difference between the `useMemo` and `useCallback` hooks?
options: `useMemo` stores layout elements; `useCallback` tracks component network states
`useMemo` returns a memoized value result from a function; `useCallback` returns a memoized copy of the function instance itself
`useMemo` is for class component contexts; `useCallback` runs on functional hooks
There is no operational difference since their dependency arrays run identical tasks
hard.explanation: `useMemo` calls its function and caches the calculated *result* value between renders. `useCallback` caches the actual *function definition instance* itself to avoid recreation during component re-renders.

hard.question: When does React batch multiple state setter updates that occur inside a single asynchronous event handler loop?
options: React only batches state updates inside synchronous events, never asynchronous runs
React batches them together automatically into a single re-render cycle (Automatic Batching introduced in React 18)
React forces an independent layout rendering refresh sequentially for each unique setter line written
React skips state updates completely until an explicit forceUpdate event is executed
hard.explanation: Since React 18, automatic batching groups all state updates together regardless of whether they originate inside click events, promises, timeouts, or async fetch blocks, minimizing UI re-render operations.

hard.question: What structural issue can arise if you use an array loop's variable `index` as the `key` attribute prop for a list whose items can be dynamically reordered, filtered, or deleted?
options: The compiler stops execution immediately due to duplicated identity rules
It can cause severe UI rendering bugs, data mismatch corruptions, and performance hits because React relies on keys to track structural identity
The component drops its state array entirely upon reordering runs
The application runs out of memory heap allocations instantly
hard.explanation: Using array indices as keys can negatively impact performance and cause state issues with your UI if the order of items changes, since React assumes the underlying components represent the same index map parameters.

hard.question: What is the purpose of the `useImperativeHandle` hook in React application architecture?
options: To convert functional logic arrays into rigid classical structures
To customize the specific instance value or method handles exposed to a parent component when using a forwarded ref node reference
To force dynamic layout calculations to override standard CSS style cascades
To handle global network socket updates avoiding central data libraries
hard.explanation: Used alongside `forwardRef`, `useImperativeHandle` lets developers customize the precise public methods or properties that a parent component's ref can access on a child component.

hard.question: What architectural issue occurs during a React rendering process if a state update loop triggers inside the body text lines of a functional component declaration rather than inside an effect or handler hook?
options: The variable drops to an undefined evaluation silently
It triggers an infinite loop error because updating state inside a render immediately schedules a new render call
React pauses app cycles until background threads become vacant
The property scales layout boundaries out of proportion automatically
hard.explanation: Executing a state setter directly within a component's render block causes a cascading re-render loop that triggers over and over until React hits its internal safety limit and crashes with a 'Too many re-renders' error.

hard.question: What functional rule governs the placement and initialization order of React hooks inside functional components?
options: Hooks must be nested inside nested tracking loops to ensure conditional execution safety
Hooks must only be called at the top level of your component, avoiding conditionals, loops, or nested functions to ensure reliable execution order across renders
Hooks must append exclusively after JSX evaluation markup layout strings conclude
Hooks must be registered inside global window context files before components boot
hard.explanation: React relies on the absolute order of Hook calls across renders. Placing hooks inside loops or conditionals breaks this ordering sequence and leads to unexpected state calculation bugs.

hard.question: What occurs when a component uses the `useTransition` hook introduced to handle concurrent features?
options: It speeds up animations using hardware GPU acceleration layers
It lets you mark specific state updates as non-blocking 'transitions', allowing urgent updates like typing to interrupt heavy background rendering cycles
It handles physical context transition animations across separate pages
It transforms synchronous network requests into multi-threaded sub-routines
hard.explanation: `useTransition` is a concurrent feature that lets developers prioritize urgent updates (like input fields) while allowing slower background UI calculations (like filtering lists) to process smoothly without freezing the page.

hard.question: What is the exact difference between the `useEffect` hook and the `useLayoutEffect` hook?
options: `useEffect` works inside modern browsers; `useLayoutEffect` functions solely on backend servers
`useEffect` fires asynchronously after the browser paints screen updates; `useLayoutEffect` fires synchronously *before* the browser paints the screen
`useEffect` takes no dependencies; `useLayoutEffect` demands mandatory state array parameters
There is no actual operational variance because compilers optimize their code blocks identically
hard.explanation: `useLayoutEffect` executes synchronously after all DOM mutations but before the browser paints the screen visually. This prevents visual flickering when measuring or adjusting DOM layout metrics manually.

hard.question: How does the `Suspense` component work in modern React when handling asynchronous data fetching components?
options: It forces standard components to stop rendering tracking states across all network nodes
It lets a component tree coordinate loading states, rendering a fallback UI until children components finish loading data or code resources
It pauses browser background execution threads to optimize CPU memory usage limits
It encrypts lazy component chunks to guarantee safe transport over standard web routes
hard.explanation: `Suspense` allows you to orchestrate orchestrate loading sequences by catching components that are 'suspending' (waiting for async resources like data or lazy-loaded code chunks) and cleanly rendering a loader fallback.

easy.question: Which of these is NOT a frontend technology?
easy.explanation: Node.js is a backend/runtime environment; HTML, CSS, and JavaScript are core frontend technologies.

easy.question: What is the primary function of a web browser?
options: To store server database records permanently
To fetch web resources and compile/render them into a visual interface for the user
To compile source code into machine executable desktop files
easy.explanation: A web browser receives HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files from a server and parses them into a visible, interactive user interface.

easy.question: Which HTTP status code signifies that a requested resource was not found on the server?
easy.explanation: The 404 status code tells the client/browser that the server could not find the specific requested URL path.

easy.question: What does DOM stand for in frontend development workflows?
easy.explanation: DOM stands for Document Object Model, which represents the hierarchical structural tree of an HTML document as object nodes.

easy.question: Which of these formats is a widely supported text-based format for exchanging data between frontends and backend APIs?
easy.explanation: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight, human-readable format used extensively for transferring structured data over HTTP.

easy.question: What design strategy focuses on making web layouts scale gracefully across different device screen dimensions (mobile, tablet, desktop)?
easy.explanation: Responsive Web Design utilizes flexible layouts, fluid grids, and CSS media queries to optimize UI visibility across variable screens.

easy.question: Which structural tool helps web developers inspect HTML elements, debug JavaScript execution, and analyze network payloads directly inside the browser?
easy.explanation: Modern browsers include built-in Developer Tools (DevTools) that give developers debugging pipelines for DOM, styles, and scripts.

easy.question: Which protocol provides encrypted, secure communication over a computer network for modern websites?
easy.explanation: HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) encrypts data transfers using TLS/SSL to safeguard user privacy and transactional integrity.

easy.question: What is the primary function of the package manager 'npm' in a frontend developer environment?
options: To optimize image assets during production deployments
To install, share, and manage open-source third-party dependencies and code modules
To automatically generate semantic CSS styles from text specifications
To execute automated database migration queries safely
easy.explanation: npm (Node Package Manager) allows developers to install libraries, tools, and frameworks from a global registry into local JavaScript codebases.

easy.question: What structural component stores web data locally directly inside a user's browser with no absolute expiration date criteria?
easy.explanation: LocalStorage saves key-value string records locally in the web browser. The data persists indefinitely even after the browser window closes.

medium.question: What does CORS stand for in web development?
medium.explanation: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a security feature that controls how resources on a web page can be requested from another domain.

medium.question: What is the main operational difference between a Single Page Application (SPA) and a traditional Multi-Page Application (MPA)?
options: SPAs do not run any JavaScript logic inside client contexts
SPAs load a single HTML document and update components dynamically via client-side routing, avoiding full page refreshes
MPAs cannot communicate with third-party backend API servers
SPAs require independent local software installations on end-user machines
medium.explanation: SPAs rewrite the existing page view dynamically instead of downloading entirely new HTML documents from a server on every link click.

medium.question: What structural role does a build bundler tool like Vite or Webpack play in modern frontend code pipelines?
options: It hosts operational production production relational database records
It parses, processes, and bundles modular code files, assets, and dependencies into consolidated static production assets
It routes live server socket traffic to dynamic load balancers
It translates javascript files directly into raw hardware firmware scripts
medium.explanation: Bundlers scan dependency trees to compress and bundle modular JavaScript, CSS, and images into highly optimized static files for the browser.

medium.question: What occurs when a browser executes a 'preflight request' under CORS security rules?
options: The client fetches a cached version of the page UI markup structure
The browser sends an HTTP OPTIONS request to the target server to check if cross-origin communication is safely permitted before sending the actual payload
The browser wipes local cookies to prevent data validation leakage vectors
The server forces the frontend app to download a security certificate layout file
medium.explanation: For complex HTTP requests (like custom headers or non-standard methods), the browser automatically triggers a preflight OPTIONS request to verify cross-origin safety clearances.

medium.question: What is the purpose of Semantic HTML tags (like <nav>, <main>, <header>) in modern web interfaces?
options: They speed up image asset compression and layout calculations
They provide explicit structural meaning to the markup, enhancing both web accessibility (a11y) and search engine optimization (SEO)
They eliminate the need to write external CSS rule declarations
They compile code structures into secure sandbox frames autonomously
medium.explanation: Semantic tags give explicit meaning to web content, allowing screen readers and search crawlers to accurately parse the structure and hierarchy of a document.

medium.question: What happens when you implement the performance technique known as 'Lazy Loading' to image elements?
options: Images drop to low-resolution thumbnail views forever to save bandwidth
The browser delays downloading image assets until they are about to scroll into the active viewport layout area
Images compress asynchronously using multi-threaded local algorithms
The server blocks image access unless the client passes valid token details
medium.explanation: Lazy loading optimizes initial page load speed by deferring the loading of non-critical off-screen media assets until they are needed.

medium.question: What design challenge is directly solved by incorporating Polyfills into client-side asset compilation setups?
options: They prevent external API endpoints from dropping active network sockets
They provide modern JavaScript functionality fallback code implementations inside legacy browsers that do not natively support those features
They minimize the overall storage foot-print of structural relational database setups
They enforce specific user validation passwords inside input fields autonomously
medium.explanation: Polyfills run a code check at runtime to patch missing browser features, ensuring modern web applications don't break on older browser versions.

medium.question: What is the primary function of a Content Delivery Network (CDN) in web application deployment topologies?
options: To run real-time server database normalization tasks
To distribute and cache static frontend assets across a geographically dispersed network of proxy servers, minimizing latency for global users
To encrypt private repository configuration keys against developer mistakes
To compile web template elements into native desktop software applications
medium.explanation: CDNs cache static assets (HTML, CSS, JS, images) on edge servers close to the user's geographical location, radically reducing travel time for network packets.

medium.question: What is the core functional difference between sessionStorage and localStorage configurations?
options: sessionStorage holds raw objects; localStorage only manages string matrices
sessionStorage records clear automatically when the specific browser tab or session terminates; localStorage data persists across browser reboots
sessionStorage synchronizes values with external cloud storage paths in real-time
localStorage restricts storage limits to a tiny 4KB max payload threshold parameters
medium.explanation: While both offer local client-side storage, sessionStorage isolates its storage lifecycle exclusively to the active tab session duration, wiping data when the tab is closed.

medium.question: What structural role do 'CSS Media Queries' execute inside responsive web style frameworks?
options: They query remote backend databases to determine what specific theme values a user prefers
They apply unique blocks of CSS layout styles only if the user's device screen meets specific criteria (such as minimum width bounds)
They automate javascript code bundle optimization workflows based on bandwidth constraints
They track interactive animation keyframes avoiding rendering errors
medium.explanation: Media queries are a core component of responsive design, telling the browser to toggle explicit design rules based on target viewport width, height, or orientation states.

hard.question: Which HTTP header is used to prevent clickjacking attacks?
hard.explanation: X-Frame-Options prevents a page from being embedded in an iframe, mitigating clickjacking attacks.

hard.question: What architectural pipeline describes the Critical Rendering Path (CRP) sequential steps executed by a browser?
options: Fetch API -> Route State -> Normalize Data -> Render Canvas UI
Parse HTML -> Build DOM -> Parse CSS -> Build CSSOM -> Combine into Render Tree -> Run Layout -> Paint Pixels
Compile JavaScript -> Map Memory -> Run Garbage Collection -> Build UI Views
Load Service Worker -> Intercept Cache -> Mutate Proxy States -> Repaint Layout Frame
hard.explanation: The Critical Rendering Path maps the precise structural milestones a browser traverses to process HTML, CSS, and JS into actual physical pixels visible on screen displays.

hard.question: What specific security vulnerability does the implementation of a strict Content Security Policy (CSP) header primarily mitigate?
options: SQL Injection vectors on backend transactional databases
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) and data injection exploits by restricting authorized asset sources
DDoS saturation traffic directed at load balancers
Brute-force password guessing patterns inside authorization blocks
hard.explanation: A CSP header tells the browser which specific domains, scripts, images, and connection protocols are legitimate, blocking unauthorized malicious tracking injections from running.

hard.question: How does a Service Worker intercept and handle network requests within a Progressive Web App (PWA) context?
options: It operates on the UI thread, blocking user events until network requests wrap up
It acts as a background network proxy layer running separate from the main page thread, reading, modifying, or serving requests straight from custom caches
It opens continuous database queries against remote server endpoints directly
It rewrites the CSS style tree whenever network latency limits are crossed
hard.explanation: Service Workers run on separate background threads. They act as network proxies, allowing developers to programmatically intercept requests to enable offline support and advanced caching strategies.

hard.question: What is the key functional difference between a web browser's 'reflow' (Layout) process and 'repaint' process?
options: Reflow alters the text characters; repaint tweaks image layout paths exclusively
Reflow calculates the geometry, size, and positioning dimensions of elements; repaint recalculates and draws visual styles like colors or shadows without changing structural geometry
Repaint handles asynchronous data requests; reflow targets synchronous interface elements
There is no difference; browsers use the terms interchangeably for all DOM modifications
hard.explanation: Reflow (Layout) is computationally expensive because changing geometry forces the browser to recalculate bounds for adjacent or parent elements. Repaint only updates aesthetic changes like visibility or color background shifts.

hard.question: What is the core structural purpose of the 'Hydration' process when working inside Server-Side Rendering (SSR) or Static Site Generation (SSG) web architectures?
options: To compress bundle payload structures before transmitting assets over the wire
To attach client-side JavaScript event listeners to a static HTML structure that was already rendered and delivered by the server
To safely wipe browser local caches to free up system memory
To convert text objects into relational database schemas dynamically
hard.explanation: SSR frameworks deliver pre-rendered, static HTML to the user for fast initial page load. 'Hydration' takes over on the client side, binding JavaScript event listeners to that static layout to make it interactive.

hard.question: Which of the following optimization techniques describes 'Tree Shaking' inside modern build compilation workflows?
options: An automated script that restructures database records to optimize lookups
A dead-code elimination process that analyzes ES module imports/exports to drop unused JavaScript code from final production bundles
A security check that tests third-party node packages for malware vulnerabilities
An asset compiler that auto-generates responsive alternate image width distributions
hard.explanation: Tree shaking acts as a bundler optimization. By analyzing static `import` and `export` statements, it strips away unused paths and dead code, dramatically shrinking the final bundle size.

hard.question: What security vulnerability is introduced if a frontend application injects unvalidated user string data directly into a DOM node using the innerHTML property?
hard.explanation: Using `innerHTML` unsafely allows malicious users to insert and execute arbitrary script tags inside another user's browser, creating a high-severity Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability.

hard.question: What core design advantage does the HTTP/2 multiplexing feature deliver to modern frontend application asset management?
options: It automatically converts standard string data arrays into compressed binary files
It allows browsers to request and download multiple file assets simultaneously over a single persistent TCP connection, eliminating head-of-line blocking
It enforces strict client authorization patterns directly at the network routing layer
It completely bypasses the browser cache to pull fresh server code updates on every run
hard.explanation: Under HTTP/1.x, browsers were restricted to downloading a handful of files in parallel per connection. HTTP/2 multiplexing removes this restriction, allowing hundreds of assets to stream concurrently over a single connection.

hard.question: What specific user interaction issue is a developer resolving when implementing code logic that relies on a 'Throttling' function versus a 'Debouncing' function?
options: Throttling switches elements between hidden and visible visibility states; debouncing structures text blocks into code strings

Throttling guarantees a callback function executes periodically at a fixed maximum frequency; debouncing delays execution until a specific period of inactivity has elapsed since the last event trigger
Throttling handles secure local data encryption tasks; debouncing acts on network routing states
There are no operational differences because both use standard window time metrics
hard.explanation: Both throttle and debounce control the frequency of heavy execution triggers. Throttling limits continuous events (like scrolling) to run once per defined time window, while debouncing delays events (like typing in a search bar) until input ceases.

easy.question: Which of the following is NOT an operating system?
easy.explanation: Microsoft Word is an application software, not an operating system.

easy.question: What does 'OS' stand for?
easy.explanation: OS stands for Operating System, the software that manages hardware and software resources.

easy.question: Which of these is a mobile operating system developed by Google?
easy.explanation: Android is a mobile operating system based on a modified version of the Linux kernel, developed by Google.

easy.question: What is the process of starting a computer called?
easy.explanation: Booting is the process of powering on a computer and getting the operating system started.

easy.question: Which of these is a common desktop operating system from Apple?
easy.explanation: macOS is the proprietary operating system developed by Apple for its Mac computers.

easy.question: What is the small picture on the desktop that represents a program called?
easy.explanation: Icons are small graphical representations of programs, files, or folders on a computer's desktop.

easy.question: Which key is often used to refresh the active window or webpage in Windows?
easy.explanation: The F5 key is the standard shortcut for refreshing the current window or webpage in most Windows applications.

easy.question: Where are deleted files usually stored before being permanently removed in Windows?
easy.explanation: In Windows, deleted files are moved to the Recycle Bin, allowing for potential recovery before permanent deletion.

easy.question: What does GUI stand for?
easy.explanation: GUI stands for Graphical User Interface, which allows users to interact with devices through graphical icons and visual indicators.

easy.question: Which part of the OS allows users to manage files and folders?
easy.explanation: File Explorer (formerly Windows Explorer) is the file management application used in Windows.

medium.question: What is the primary function of an operating system's kernel?
options: Manage hardware resources and system calls
medium.explanation: The kernel is the core component that manages system resources, hardware communication, and process scheduling.

medium.question: What is the name of the 'open source' operating system that many others are based on?
medium.explanation: Linux is a family of open-source Unix-like operating systems based on the Linux kernel.

medium.question: What is 'Virtual Memory' used for in an operating system?
options: To allow the OS to use hard drive space as additional RAM
medium.explanation: Virtual memory is a memory management technique that provides an 'idealized' view of storage, using disk space to extend physical RAM.

medium.question: In Windows, which utility is used to check system performance and running processes?
medium.explanation: Task Manager provides information about computer performance and software currently running on the system.

medium.question: Which of the following is a multi-tasking operating system?
medium.explanation: Windows 10 is a modern multi-tasking OS, whereas early systems like MS-DOS were primarily single-tasking.

medium.question: What is a 'Device Driver'?
options: Hardware that connects to a computer
Software that allows the OS to communicate with hardware
A program to clean the registry
medium.explanation: A device driver is a specialized software program that controls a particular type of hardware device attached to a computer.

medium.question: Which of these is the core part of an OS that manages CPU and memory?
medium.explanation: The Kernel is the central component of an operating system that manages system resources and hardware/software communication.

medium.question: What is 'Spooling' primarily used for?
medium.explanation: Spooling (Simultaneous Peripheral Operations On-Line) is commonly used to manage data being sent to printers.

medium.question: Which command-line interface is native to Windows for advanced scripting and automation?
medium.explanation: PowerShell is a task automation and configuration management framework from Microsoft, consisting of a command-line shell and scripting language.

medium.question: What happens during a 'Page Fault'?
options: The OS fails to find requested data in RAM and must fetch it from the disk
medium.explanation: A page fault occurs when a program tries to access data in memory that is not currently in physical RAM, forcing the OS to retrieve it from secondary storage.

hard.question: In Unix/Linux, which command is used to change file permissions?
hard.explanation: chmod (change mode) is used to modify file permissions in Unix-like systems.

hard.question: What is 'Thrashing' in the context of operating systems?
options: A state where the CPU spends more time swapping pages than executing processes
hard.explanation: Thrashing occurs when the virtual memory subsystem is in a constant state of paging, leading to low CPU productivity.

hard.question: What is a 'Deadlock' in process management?
options: A situation where two or more processes are waiting for each other to release resources
hard.explanation: A deadlock is a specific condition where a set of processes are blocked because each process is holding a resource and waiting for another resource held by another process.

hard.question: Which scheduling algorithm gives each process a fixed time slot in a cyclic way?
hard.explanation: Round Robin is a preemptive scheduling algorithm where each process is assigned a fixed time unit called a quantum.

hard.question: What is the purpose of the 'Dirty Bit' in memory management?
options: To indicate that a page has been modified since it was loaded from disk
hard.explanation: A dirty bit (or modified bit) is a flag that indicates whether the corresponding page of memory has been modified by software.

hard.question: In a Unix-like system, what is the 'PID' of the 'init' process (the first process started)?
hard.explanation: The 'init' process (or systemd in modern systems) is always assigned Process ID (PID) 1.

hard.question: What does 'SJF' stand for in CPU scheduling?
hard.explanation: Shortest Job First (SJF) is a scheduling policy that selects the process with the smallest execution time to run next.

hard.question: What is a 'Context Switch'?
options: The process of storing and restoring the state of a CPU so that execution can be resumed from the same point later
hard.explanation: A context switch is the procedure that a computer's CPU follows to change from one task (or process) to another while ensuring that the tasks do not conflict.

hard.question: Which of these is a 'Microkernel' based operating system?
hard.explanation: MINIX is a famous example of a microkernel architecture, where the kernel provides minimal services.

hard.question: What is the 'Banker's Algorithm' used for?
hard.explanation: The Banker's algorithm is a resource allocation and deadlock avoidance algorithm that tests for safety by simulating the allocation of predetermined maximum possible amounts of all resources.

easy.question: What does LAN stand for?
easy.explanation: LAN stands for Local Area Network, used to connect devices in a limited area like a home or office.

easy.question: What does WAN stand for?
easy.explanation: WAN stands for Wide Area Network, which covers a large geographic area like a country or the entire world.

easy.question: Which device is used to connect multiple computers in a local network?
easy.explanation: A network switch is used to connect devices on a computer network by using packet switching to receive and forward data.

easy.question: What is the primary function of a Router?
options: To connect different networks and direct traffic
easy.explanation: A router is a networking device that forwards data packets between computer networks.

easy.question: What does 'Wi-Fi' stand for?
easy.explanation: Wi-Fi is a family of wireless network protocols based on the IEEE 802.11 family of standards.

easy.question: Which of these is a unique identifier assigned to a network interface controller?
easy.explanation: A MAC (Media Access Control) address is a unique identifier assigned to a network interface for communications at the data link layer.

easy.question: What does HTTP stand for?
easy.explanation: HTTP stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol, the foundation of data communication for the World Wide Web.

easy.question: Which device converts digital signals to analog signals for transmission over telephone lines?
easy.explanation: A modem (modulator-demodulator) converts digital data to analog signals and vice versa.

easy.question: What is the standard port for HTTP?
easy.explanation: Port 80 is the default port used for unencrypted HTTP traffic.

easy.question: What does DNS stand for?
easy.explanation: DNS stands for Domain Name System, which translates human-readable domain names (like google.com) into IP addresses.

medium.question: Which protocol is used to send emails?
medium.explanation: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is the standard protocol for sending emails across networks.

medium.question: Which layer of the OSI model is responsible for routing data packets?
medium.explanation: The Network Layer (Layer 3) handles routing, packet forwarding, and logical addressing (IP).

medium.question: What is the length of an IPv4 address?
medium.explanation: An IPv4 address is 32 bits long, typically written in dotted-decimal notation (e.g., 192.168.1.1).

medium.question: What is the length of an IPv6 address?
medium.explanation: An IPv6 address is 128 bits long, providing a much larger address space than IPv4.

medium.question: Which protocol is considered 'connectionless'?
medium.explanation: UDP (User Datagram Protocol) is connectionless, meaning it sends data without establishing a formal connection first.

medium.question: What does DHCP stand for?
medium.explanation: DHCP is used to automatically assign IP addresses and other network configurations to devices on a network.

medium.question: In a Star topology, all devices are connected to a central device called a:
medium.explanation: In a star network topology, every peripheral node is connected to a central hub or switch.

medium.question: Which protocol is used for securely transferring files between a client and a server?
medium.explanation: SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol) provides secure file access, transfer, and management over a reliable data stream.

medium.question: What is the purpose of a Firewall?
options: To monitor and control incoming/outgoing network traffic based on security rules
medium.explanation: A firewall acts as a barrier between a trusted internal network and untrusted external networks.

medium.question: Which protocol is used to retrieve emails from a server and usually deletes them from the server once downloaded?
medium.explanation: POP3 (Post Office Protocol version 3) is used to retrieve emails, typically downloading them to the local device and removing them from the server.

hard.question: What is the default port number for HTTPS?
hard.explanation: HTTPS uses port 443 by default for secure web communication.

hard.question: Which layer of the OSI model handles data encryption and compression?
hard.explanation: The Presentation Layer (Layer 6) is responsible for data translation, encryption, and compression.

hard.question: What is the 'Three-Way Handshake' used in?
hard.explanation: TCP uses a three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) to establish a reliable connection between a client and a server.

hard.question: What does BGP stand for in internet routing?
hard.explanation: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is the standardized exterior gateway protocol designed to exchange routing and reachability information between autonomous systems.

hard.question: What is 'Subnetting'?
options: Increasing the size of a network
Dividing a large network into smaller, manageable sub-networks
Connecting two different types of networks
hard.explanation: Subnetting is the practice of dividing a network into two or more smaller networks to improve performance and security.

hard.question: Which protocol is used by the 'ping' command to test network connectivity?
hard.explanation: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) is used by network devices to send error messages and operational information, such as ping requests.

hard.question: What is the range of valid private IP addresses in Class C?
hard.explanation: The Class C private IP range is 192.168.0.0 to 192.168.255.255.

hard.question: What does 'TTL' stand for in an IP packet?
hard.explanation: TTL (Time To Live) is a value in an IP packet that tells a network router whether or not the packet has been in the network too long and should be discarded.

hard.question: Which of these is a tunneling protocol used to create a VPN connection?
hard.explanation: L2TP (Layer 2 Tunneling Protocol) is a tunneling protocol used to support virtual private networks (VPNs).

hard.question: What is 'CSMA/CD' used for in Ethernet networks?
hard.explanation: CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) is a media access control method used to handle collisions in half-duplex Ethernet networks.

easy.question: Which keyboard shortcut is used to 'Copy' selected text in most applications?
easy.explanation: Ctrl + C copies selected content to the clipboard. Ctrl + V pastes, Ctrl + X cuts, and Ctrl + Z undoes.

easy.question: Which shortcut is used to 'Paste' content from the clipboard?
easy.explanation: Ctrl + V is the standard shortcut to paste content that has been copied or cut.

easy.question: What is the keyboard shortcut to 'Undo' the last action?
easy.explanation: Ctrl + Z undoes the previous action in most software.

easy.question: Which shortcut 'Saves' the current document or file?
easy.explanation: Ctrl + S is the universal shortcut for saving the current file.

easy.question: What is the shortcut to 'Select All' items or text?
easy.explanation: Ctrl + A selects all content in the active window or document.

easy.question: Which shortcut is used to 'Cut' selected text or items?
easy.explanation: Ctrl + X removes the selected content and places it on the clipboard.

easy.question: Which shortcut opens the 'Print' dialog?
easy.explanation: Ctrl + P is used to bring up the print options in most applications.

easy.question: What does Alt + F4 do in Windows?
options: Closes the active window or program
easy.explanation: Alt + F4 is the standard shortcut to close the currently active application window.

easy.question: Which shortcut toggles 'Bold' formatting on selected text?
easy.explanation: Ctrl + B is used to bold or unbold selected text in word processors and text editors.

easy.question: Which key is used to cancel an operation or close a popup menu?
easy.explanation: The Esc (Escape) key is commonly used to cancel current tasks or close dialog boxes.

medium.question: What does Ctrl + Shift + Esc open in Windows?
medium.explanation: Ctrl + Shift + Esc directly opens the Task Manager in Windows.

medium.question: What is the shortcut to permanently delete a file in Windows (bypassing the Recycle Bin)?
medium.explanation: Shift + Delete permanently deletes a file without moving it to the Recycle Bin.

medium.question: Which shortcut 'Redoes' an action that was undone?
medium.explanation: Ctrl + Y (or Ctrl + Shift + Z in some apps) is used to redo an action that was previously undone.

medium.question: What is the shortcut to switch between open applications in Windows?
medium.explanation: Alt + Tab allows users to quickly cycle through and switch between open application windows.

medium.question: Which shortcut takes a screenshot of the active window only?
medium.explanation: Alt + Print Screen captures only the currently active window to the clipboard.

medium.question: Which shortcut opens the 'Find' or 'Search' tool in most browsers?
medium.explanation: Ctrl + F opens the search bar to find specific text on a page or document.

medium.question: What does Windows Key + L do?
medium.explanation: Windows Key + L instantly locks the PC and displays the lock screen.

medium.question: Which shortcut reloads the current page and clears the cache in most browsers?
medium.explanation: Ctrl + F5 (or Cmd + Shift + R on Mac) performs a 'hard' reload, clearing the cache for that page.

medium.question: Which shortcut opens the 'Run' dialog box in Windows?
medium.explanation: Windows + R opens the Run command box for launching programs and scripts.

medium.question: What does Ctrl + Shift + T do in a web browser?
medium.explanation: Ctrl + Shift + T restores the most recently closed tab or window in browsers like Chrome and Firefox.

hard.question: In VS Code, which shortcut opens the Command Palette?
hard.explanation: Ctrl + Shift + P (or Cmd + Shift + P on Mac) opens the Command Palette in Visual Studio Code.

hard.question: In Chrome Developer Tools, which shortcut toggles the device toolbar for mobile emulation?
hard.explanation: Ctrl + Shift + M toggles the device toolbar to test responsive designs.

hard.question: What is the shortcut to open the 'Advanced Boot Options' or 'Project' menu in Windows 10/11?
hard.explanation: Windows + P opens the projection settings for multiple monitors.

hard.question: In VS Code, which shortcut is used for 'Multi-cursor' selection at specific points?
hard.explanation: Alt + Click (or Option + Click on Mac) allows you to place multiple cursors in different locations.

hard.question: Which shortcut 'Minimizes' all open windows and shows the desktop?
hard.explanation: Both Windows + D and Windows + M show the desktop, though Windows + D toggles it.

hard.question: In Excel, which key is used to edit the content of the active cell?
hard.explanation: Pressing F2 enters the edit mode for the selected cell.

hard.question: What is the shortcut to open 'Windows Sandbox' (if enabled)?
hard.explanation: Windows Sandbox does not have a default global keyboard shortcut; it must be launched from the Start menu or via a custom shortcut.

hard.question: In many IDEs, what does Ctrl + / do?
hard.explanation: Ctrl + / is the standard shortcut to comment or uncomment the current line of code.

hard.question: In Linux Terminal (Bash), which shortcut kills the current foreground process?
